en.extract:
The brown-backed parrotlet (Touit melanonotus) also known as the black-backed parrotlet, the black-eared parrotlet, and Wied's parrotlet, is a small (15 cm or 6 in) green parrot found in south-eastern Brazil from Bahia to southern São Paulo. It has a dark brown mantle and back, brown ear coverts, and red outer tail with back tips. They frequent humid forest from 500–1,000 m (1,600–3,300 ft) (occasionally down to sea level), and are mostly found in small flocks of 3–20 birds.


Ecology
It is mostly known from lower montane evergreen forest at 500–1,200 metres (1,600–3,900 ft), but also up to 1,400 m (4,600 ft) in the Itatiaia National Park. In addition it is found in near sea-level in Bahia and São Paulo. Seasonal migration or dispersal is suspected, though this may amount to little more than short altitudinal movements. Food items are poorly studied but include large leguminous seeds, fruit of Rapanea acuminata, Clusia sp. and mistletoes. 
Observed feeding on Clusia criuva where presumed adults pluck the fruit from the tree snipping the stalk with their bills and carry the fruit to a more secure location on a firmer branch where they open the fruitwith their bill wedging it against the branch prising the seeds out with their tongue; they do not use their feet at any time in the feeding process as some other psittacids do.  Some individuals that were presumed to be young birds were seen to eat the fruits in situ and did not pluck them. 
Breeding is suspected to occur from September to October. Well grown young birds were observed being fed by adults in Ubatuba, São Paulo state, January 2010. Young birds can be differentiated by their paler orbital ring and their cere being flesh coloured as opposed to dark grey in adults.


Range and population
The brown-backed parrotlet is confined to south-eastern Brazil (Bahia, Espírito Santo, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo), but is a vagrant to Rio de Janeiro, and from São Paulo to south to Ilha do Cardoso. They were resighted in Bahia after a century long absence. They were never deemed common, but seen as rather rare, even in the 19th century. Their inconspicuous nature and naturally low densities may contribute to the paucity of records. A resurgence in sightings since the mid-1980s, and their discovery at Espírito Santo, was due to knowledge of their calls.


References

Juniper & Parr (1998) Parrots: A Guide to Parrots of the World; ISBN 0-300-07453-0.
BirdLife Species Factsheet


External links
World Parrot Trust Parrot Encyclopedia – Species Profile

The '''brown-backed parrotlet''' ('''''Touit melanonotus''''') also known as the '''black-backed parrotlet''', the '''black-eared parrotlet''', and '''Wied's parrotlet''', is a small ({{convert|15|cm|0|disp=or|abbr=on}}) green [[parrot]] found in south-eastern [[Brazil]] from [[Bahia]] to southern [[São Paulo (state)|São Paulo]]. It has a dark brown mantle and back, brown ear coverts, and red outer tail with back tips. They frequent humid forest from {{convert|500|-|1000|m|abbr=on}} (occasionally down to sea level), and are mostly found in small flocks of 3–20 birds.

==Ecology==
It is mostly known from lower montane evergreen forest at {{convert|500|-|1200|m}}, but also up to {{convert|1400|m|abbr=on}} in the [[Itatiaia National Park]]. In addition it is found in near sea-level in Bahia and São Paulo.<ref>F. Olmos in litt. (1999) {{citation needed|date=January 2012}}</ref><ref name="WL">{{cite book |author=David C. Wege |author2=Adrian J. Long |name-list-style=amp |year=1995 |title=Key Areas for Threatened Birds in the Neotropics |publisher=[[BirdLife International]] |location=Cambridge, U.K. |series=BirdLife Conservation Series |volume=05 |isbn=978-0-946888-31-3}}</ref><ref name="SS">{{cite journal |author=R. Simpson |author2=E, Simpson |name-list-style=amp|year=2012 |title=Behavioural notes on Brown-backed Parrotlet ''Touit melenonotus'' in Ubatuba, Sao Paulo, Brazil|journal=[[Cotinga (journal)|Cotinga]] |volume=34 |pages=19–23}}</ref> Seasonal migration or dispersal is suspected, though this may amount to little more than short altitudinal movements. Food items are poorly studied but include large [[Fabaceae|leguminous]] seeds, fruit of ''[[Rapanea acuminata]]'', ''[[Clusia]]'' sp. and [[mistletoe]]s.<ref name="WO">{{cite journal |author=E. O. Willis |author2=Y. Oniki |name-list-style=amp |year=1993 |title=New and reconfirmed birds from the state of São Paulo, Brazil, with notes on disappearing species |journal=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]] |volume=113 |pages=23–34}}</ref> 
Observed feeding on ''Clusia criuva'' where presumed adults pluck the fruit from the tree snipping the stalk with their bills and carry the fruit to a more secure location on a firmer branch where they open the fruitwith their bill wedging it against the branch prising the seeds out with their tongue; they do not use their feet at any time in the feeding process as some other ''psittacids'' do.  Some individuals that were presumed to be young birds were seen to eat the fruits in ''situ'' and did not pluck them.<ref name="SS"/> 
Breeding is suspected to occur from September to October.<ref>Collar (1997a) {{citation needed|date=January 2012}}</ref> Well grown young birds were observed being fed by adults in Ubatuba, São Paulo state, January 2010. Young birds can be differentiated by their paler orbital ring and their cere being flesh coloured as opposed to dark grey in adults.<ref name="SS"/>

==Range and population==
The brown-backed parrotlet is confined to south-eastern Brazil (Bahia, [[Espírito Santo]], [[Rio de Janeiro (state)|Rio de Janeiro]] and São Paulo), but is a vagrant to Rio de Janeiro, and from São Paulo to south to [[Ilha do Cardoso]].<ref name="WL"/> They were resighted in Bahia after a century long absence.<ref name="WO"/><ref>J. Minns in litt. (1999) {{citation needed|date=January 2012}}</ref> They were never deemed common, but seen as rather rare, even in the 19th century. Their inconspicuous nature and naturally low densities may contribute to the paucity of records. A resurgence in sightings since the mid-1980s, and their discovery at Espírito Santo, was due to knowledge of their calls.<ref name="WO"/>

==References==

* [http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=1636&m=0 BirdLife Species Factsheet]
